topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope lay on my desk, crisp and stark white against the muted grey of the afternoon. My thumb traced the embossed address, a slow, deliberate movement that felt clumsy and heavy. Across the paper, in bold, uncompromising letters, was the news: Liam was moving. Across the country. A sudden, profound silence descended upon the room, the usual low hum of the apartment suddenly deafening. I picked up my phone, the screen reflecting a face that wasn't mine, and stared at the contact. My fingers hovered over the call button, then retreated, the weight of the impending distance pressing down on my chest until it was hard to draw a full breath. I stood up, pacing the length of the living room, the floorboards protesting beneath my hurried steps. Every familiar object—the chipped mug, the worn armchair—seemed to hold a ghost of his presence, making the emptiness of the space feel vast and insurmountable.

[story 2]

"You're serious?" The words felt brittle, catching in my throat as I looked at the printed confirmation email. It was a simple logistical update, yet it felt like a physical blow. I reread the details three times, my eyes skipping over the coordinates and the moving date, searching for some flaw in the information, a trick of the eye. When I finally looked up, the sunlight streaming through the window seemed too bright, too sharp. I walked to the balcony, leaning against the cool railing, and watched the city sprawl below. It looked enormous, indifferent. A strange, hollow ache settled in my stomach, a cold, tight knot that radiated outward. I pulled my knees to my chest, focusing intently on the pattern of the paving stones, trying to anchor myself to something solid when everything felt like it was tilting off its axis.

[story 3]

I kept rereading the text message, the same three sentences repeating themselves, each iteration landing with a dull thud in my mind. It wasn't just a change of address; it was an amputation, the sudden severing of a daily reality I had built around him. My hands began to tremble, a fine vibration that I fought to suppress by gripping the edge of the table until my knuckles were white. I paced back and forth in the kitchen, the sound of my own footsteps echoing unnervingly in the quiet space. I stopped abruptly, staring at the half-finished cup of coffee cooling on the counter. The steam had long dissipated, leaving behind a faint, bitter scent. I felt a strange pressure building behind my eyes, a desperate need to just turn around and run, but my feet remained rooted to the spot, paralyzed by the sheer, impossible scope of the separation.
